agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of a delivery platform's core domain model from a 1:1 Shipment-to-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship. This is a fundamental structural change to the Shipment aggregate root, affecting data models, APIs, partner integrations, and operational workflows. The change must preserve 2 million existing shipments while enabling multi-recipient capabilities going forward.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management — the core bounded context responsible for modeling shipments, recipients, delivery preferences, and their lifecycle from creation through final delivery.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A logistical unit representing goods being transported from an origin to one or more destinations. The aggregate root of this bounded context."},{"term":"Recipient","definition":"A party designated to receive a delivery within a Shipment. Characterized by name, address, phone number, and delivery preferences."},{"term":"Delivery Preferences","definition":"Recipient-specific instructions governing how and when delivery should occur (e.g., time windows, signature requirements, safe-place authorization, contactless delivery)."},{"term":"Multi-Recipient Shipment","definition":"A Shipment associated with more than one Recipient, where each Recipient may have distinct delivery addresses and preferences."},{"term":"Shipment Status","definition":"The lifecycle state of a Shipment. In a multi-recipient model, status semantics must account for partial completion (some recipients delivered, others pending)."},{"term":"Primary Recipient","definition":"(Proposed) The first or designated main Recipient on a multi-recipient Shipment, potentially used for billing, notifications, and backward compatibility."},{"term":"Partner API","definition":"External integrations that consume shipment data in the current single-recipient format."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have at least one Recipient at all times.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A Shipment with zero Recipients has no delivery destination and is logically invalid — it represents a corrupt state that cannot be fulfilled.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to create a Shipment with zero Recipients or remove the last Recipient from an existing Shipment must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Each Recipient within a Shipment must have a valid name, address, and phone number.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"These are the minimum data required to physically execute a delivery. Without any one of them, the courier cannot complete the delivery.","testable_assertion":"Creating or updating a Recipient with a null/empty name, address, or phone number must be rejected with a validation error."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Each Recipient within a Shipment owns their own independent delivery preferences.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Delivery preferences are intrinsically tied to a specific Recipient and address — sharing them across Recipients would produce incorrect delivery behavior.","testable_assertion":"Updating delivery preferences for Recipient A on a multi-recipient Shipment must not alter Recipient B's preferences."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Existing single-recipient Shipments must remain valid and fully operational after the model migration without any data loss or behavioral change.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"2 million production records and their dependent workflows cannot be broken. Backward compatibility is a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"After migration, all 2 million existing Shipments must be queryable and return their original Recipient data intact, with no field loss or semantic change."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Shipment status must reflect the aggregate delivery state across all Recipients.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A Shipment cannot be marked 'Delivered' if any Recipient's delivery is still pending — this would corrupt fulfillment tracking and SLA reporting.","testable_assertion":"A multi-recipient Shipment is 'Delivered' only when ALL Recipients have been successfully delivered to. If any Recipient is pending or failed, the Shipment status must reflect partial completion."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"External partner APIs must continue to receive shipment data in a backward-compatible format during the transition period.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a contractual and operational policy, not a data integrity constraint. Partners can be migrated on a negotiated timeline.","testable_assertion":"Partner API payloads for single-recipient Shipments must be identical to the current format. Multi-recipient Shipments must either be decomposed or sent with a clearly versioned schema."},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"A Recipient can appear only once per Shipment (uniqueness by address or identity).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Duplicate Recipients on the same Shipment would cause double-delivery attempts, wasted operational costs, and customer confusion.","testable_assertion":"Adding a Recipient with the same address (or same name+phone) to a Shipment that already contains that Recipient must be rejected."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"the creator specifies two or more Recipients, each with valid name, address, phone, and delivery preferences","then":"the Shipment is created successfully with all Recipients persisted and independently queryable"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A new Shipment is being created","when":"the creator specifies zero Recipients","then":"the system rejects creation with a validation error stating at least one Recipient is required"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"An existing single-recipient Shipment from before the migration","when":"it is queried via the API","then":"it returns correctly with its single Recipient, and the response is structurally consistent with the new multi-recipient model (e.g., Recipients as an array of one)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment where Recipient A has been delivered but Recipient B is pending","when":"the Shipment status is queried","then":"the status reflects 'Partially Delivered' (or equivalent), not 'Delivered'"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment","when":"delivery preferences are updated for one Recipient","then":"only that Recipient's preferences change; all other Recipients' preferences remain unchanged"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"An external partner integration consuming shipment data","when":"a single-recipient Shipment is sent via the partner API","then":"the payload format is identical to the pre-migration format (backward compatible)"},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An external partner integration consuming shipment data","when":"a multi-recipient Shipment is sent via the partner API","then":"the system either sends one event per Recipient or uses a versioned schema that the partner has acknowledged supporting"},{"id":"AC-8","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment","when":"a user attempts to remove the last remaining Recipient","then":"the operation is rejected, preserving the minimum-one-Recipient invariant"},{"id":"AC-9","given":"A multi-recipient Shipment with Recipient A at 123 Main St","when":"a user attempts to add another Recipient with the same address","then":"the system rejects the duplicate with a clear error message"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Migration of 2 million existing shipments to the new schema","expected_behavior":"Must be handled as a non-breaking, backward-compatible migration. Existing rows gain an array wrapper or join-table entry without altering semantics. Zero-downtime migration required — no 'big bang' cutover."},{"scenario":"A multi-recipient Shipment where one Recipient's delivery fails permanently","expected_behavior":"The Shipment must surface a terminal partial-failure state. Business must define whether the entire Shipment fails or only the failed Recipient is marked failed while others proceed."},{"scenario":"Cancellation of a multi-recipient Shipment after some Recipients have been delivered","expected_behavior":"Cannot un-deliver already-completed deliveries. Cancellation should apply only to pending Recipients. The Shipment enters a mixed terminal state."},{"scenario":"Partner API receives a multi-recipient Shipment before they support the new format","expected_behavior":"The integration layer must either decompose into multiple single-recipient payloads or queue until the partner upgrades. Silent data loss or truncation to first Recipient is unacceptable."},{"scenario":"Billing and pricing for multi-recipient Shipments","expected_behavior":"Pricing model must account for multiple drop-offs. A multi-recipient Shipment is not the same cost as a single-recipient one. Billing must be per-Recipient or per-Shipment with a surcharge — this needs product definition."},{"scenario":"Notifications for multi-recipient Shipments","expected_behavior":"Each Recipient should receive their own delivery notifications with their own tracking context, not a single notification about the whole Shipment."},{"scenario":"Recipient ordering and delivery sequencing","expected_behavior":"If delivery order matters (route optimization), the system must support an explicit sequence. If order is arbitrary, this should be documented. Couriers need to know which stop is next."},{"scenario":"Maximum number of Recipients per Shipment","expected_behavior":"An upper bound must be defined to prevent abuse and operational infeasibility (e.g., a Shipment with 10,000 Recipients). This is a policy, not yet defined."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Multi-recipient Shipments enable new revenue streams (group deliveries, corporate distribution, multi-stop routes) but require pricing model changes. Incorrect billing for multi-recipient Shipments could cause revenue leakage. Migration costs are non-trivial for 2M records.","operational":"Courier dispatching, route optimization, and delivery tracking all become significantly more complex. A Shipment that was previously one stop is now potentially N stops. Warehouse operations, proof-of-delivery workflows, and SLA tracking must all evolve. Partial delivery states add operational edge cases for customer support.","user":"Senders gain flexibility to ship to multiple Recipients in one transaction. Recipients get personalized delivery preferences. However, UX complexity increases — status tracking for a multi-recipient Shipment is harder to communicate clearly. Courier apps must show per-Recipient stop information.","risk":"HIGH — This is a change to the core aggregate root of the system. Risks include: (1) data migration failure affecting 2M records, (2) partner API breakage causing integration failures, (3) inconsistent status semantics causing SLA miscalculations, (4) billing errors from undefined multi-recipient pricing, (5) performance degradation from 1:N joins on high-traffic queries.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Senders/Shippers","impact":"Gain multi-recipient capability but must learn new UX. Existing single-recipient workflows must remain unchanged."},{"group":"Recipients","impact":"Each Recipient gets independent delivery preferences and notifications. Positive impact if implemented correctly."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Significant workflow change — a single Shipment now means multiple stops. Route planning, proof-of-delivery, and handoff processes must all adapt."},{"group":"External Partner Integrations","impact":"Must handle new data format. Risk of breakage if not properly versioned. Requires partner communication and migration timeline."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Must understand and troubleshoot partial delivery states, per-Recipient issues within a single Shipment. Training and tooling updates required."},{"group":"Finance/Billing","impact":"Pricing model must be redefined for multi-recipient Shipments. Revenue recognition and invoicing logic affected."},{"group":"Operations/Dispatch","impact":"Route optimization algorithms must account for multi-stop Shipments. Load planning and vehicle capacity calculations change."}]},"confidence":{"level":55,"reasoning":"The product team's requirement is clear in intent (1:N Recipients) but leaves several critical business decisions undefined: pricing model, status state machine for partial delivery, partner migration strategy, recipient uniqueness rules, and delivery sequencing. The core model change is well-understood, but the downstream business rules need product decisions.","high_confidence_areas":["The 1:N Recipient model structure is well-defined","Backward compatibility for existing 2M shipments is a hard requirement","Partner API backward compatibility is essential","At-least-one-Recipient invariant is clear","Per-Recipient delivery preferences are independently owned"],"low_confidence_areas":["Pricing and billing model for multi-recipient Shipments — not specified","Shipment status state machine for partial delivery — needs product definition","Recipient uniqueness criteria — is it by address, by name+phone, or by identity?","Delivery sequencing and route optimization impact — not addressed","Maximum Recipients per Shipment — no upper bound defined","Partner API versioning strategy — not specified","Notification strategy per Recipient — not detailed"],"assumptions":["Existing single-recipient Shipments will be wrapped into the new model (array of one) rather than maintained as a separate legacy model","Delivery preferences is a well-understood concept in the existing system and merely needs to be scoped per-Recipient","The migration can be performed online without downtime","Partner APIs have versioning capability or can tolerate a transition period"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The Shipment status lifecycle for multi-recipient Shipments is undefined. When one Recipient is delivered and another is pending, what is the Shipment's status? Is there a 'Partially Delivered' state? What happens when one Recipient delivery fails permanently — does the entire Shipment fail? The product team must define the complete status state machine for multi-recipient scenarios.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations/Dispatch","Customer Support","Senders/Shippers","Couriers/Drivers"],"options":["Introduce explicit 'Partially Delivered' and 'Partially Failed' states at the Shipment level","Track status only at the Recipient level and derive Shipment status as an aggregate","Keep Shipment status as 'In Transit' until all Recipients are resolved, then mark final state"],"recommendation":"Track delivery status independently per Recipient and derive Shipment-level status as an aggregate. This is the most domain-faithful approach — each delivery is a separate physical act. Introduce 'Partially Delivered' at the Shipment level for clear communication."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Pricing and billing model for multi-recipient Shipments is completely unspecified. Is each Recipient a separate billable delivery? Is there a base fee plus per-Recipient surcharge? Does distance between Recipients matter? Without this, the feature cannot ship — billing is a core business function.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance/Billing","Senders/Shippers"],"options":["Flat fee per Shipment regardless of Recipient count","Base fee plus per-Recipient surcharge","Fully independent pricing per Recipient as if separate Shipments","Distance-based pricing incorporating multi-stop route cost"],"recommendation":"Base fee plus per-Recipient surcharge, as it aligns with the operational reality that each additional Recipient adds marginal cost (extra stop, extra handling) but shares some fixed costs (pickup, initial transit)."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Partner API migration strategy is unspecified. External partners currently consume single-recipient format. The spec does not clarify whether to version the API, decompose multi-recipient Shipments into multiple payloads, or require partners to upgrade by a deadline.","affected_stakeholders":["External Partner Integrations","Operations/Dispatch"],"options":["API versioning with v1 (single-recipient) and v2 (multi-recipient) running in parallel","Decompose multi-recipient Shipments into N single-recipient payloads for legacy partners","Require all partners to upgrade by a hard deadline","Fan-out: send one webhook per Recipient to legacy partners"],"recommendation":"API versioning with parallel support. v1 continues to serve single-recipient format (including decomposing multi-recipient Shipments for backward compatibility). v2 supports the full multi-recipient model. Partners migrate on their own timeline with a deprecation date for v1."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Delivery preferences are mentioned but not defined. What constitutes a 'delivery preference'? Time windows, signature requirements, safe-place instructions, contactless delivery, authority-to-leave? The scope of this concept directly affects the data model and validation rules.","affected_stakeholders":["Recipients","Couriers/Drivers","Senders/Shippers"],"options":["Minimal set: time window + signature required flag","Full set: time window, signature, safe-place, contactless, special instructions, alternative Recipient","Extensible key-value model allowing arbitrary preferences"],"recommendation":"Define a bounded set of well-typed preferences (time window, signature required, safe-place authorization, special instructions) rather than an open-ended key-value bag. This ensures domain validation and courier UX clarity."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}

content.thinking: Let me analyze this domain model evolution from single-recipient to multi-recipient shipments.

trace.result: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Evolution of a delivery platform's core domain model from a 1:1 Shipment-to-Recipient relationship to a 1:N relationship. This is a fundamental structural change to the Shipment aggregate root, affecting data models, APIs, partner integrations, and operational workflows. The change must preserve 2 million existing shipments while enabling multi-recipient capabilities going forward.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management — the core bounded context responsible for modeling shipments, recipients, delivery preferences, and their lifecycle from creation through final delivery.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A logistical unit representing goods being transported from an origin to one or more destinations. The aggregate root of this bounded context."},{"term":"Recipient","definition":"A party designated to receive a delivery within a Shipment.
